items.text: Are newtypes used to distinguish domain concepts that share the same underlying type?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Newtype wrappers create zero-cost type distinctions that prevent mixing up values that have the same runtime representation (e.g., two different Text fields).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without newtypes, it is easy to pass a UserId where an OrderId is expected. The compiler catches these mistakes at zero runtime cost when newtypes are used.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for functions with multiple parameters of the same base type (Text, Int, etc.)
- Check if domain concepts like IDs, amounts, or names have their own newtypes
- Verify that newtype constructors are not exported (use smart constructors instead)
baseContent.exampleComment: This function takes two Text arguments for userId and orderId. Wrap them in newtypes so the compiler catches argument-order mistakes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
processOrder :: Text -> Text -> IO ()
processOrder userId orderId = ...

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
newtype UserId = UserId Text
newtype OrderId = OrderId Text

processOrder :: UserId -> OrderId -> IO ()
processOrder (UserId uid) (OrderId oid) = ...

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use newtypes to make illegal states unrepresentable. They cost nothing at runtime.

items.text: Are phantom types or GADTs used to encode invariants when appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Phantom type parameters and GADTs encode state-machine transitions or validation status in the type system, making invalid state transitions a compile error.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Encoding invariants at the type level eliminates entire categories of runtime errors. A validated email that is typed differently from an unvalidated one cannot be confused.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for state machines or validation workflows that could benefit from phantom types
- Check if the code uses runtime flags (isValidated :: Bool) where a type distinction would be safer
- Verify that phantom type APIs are intuitive and well-documented
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses a Bool field to track whether the form is validated. Consider using a phantom type like `Form Validated` vs `Form Unvalidated` to make invalid submissions a type error.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
data Form = Form { fields :: [Field], isValidated :: Bool }

submit :: Form -> IO Result
submit form
  | isValidated form = doSubmit form
  | otherwise = error "Form not validated"

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
data Validated
data Unvalidated

newtype Form a = Form { fields :: [Field] }

validate :: Form Unvalidated -> Either [Error] (Form Validated)
submit :: Form Validated -> IO Result  -- cannot submit unvalidated

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Encode invariants in types when the complexity is warranted. Make impossible states impossible.

items.text: Are partial functions (head, tail, fromJust) avoided in favor of total alternatives?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Partial functions crash at runtime on certain inputs (head on empty list, fromJust on Nothing). Total alternatives like pattern matching or safe versions handle all cases.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Partial functions are the Haskell equivalent of null pointer exceptions. They compile fine but crash in production on edge cases.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for head, tail, init, last, fromJust, read, (!!) in the codebase
- Check that pattern matching or safe alternatives are used instead
- Look for -Wincomplete-patterns in the build configuration
baseContent.exampleComment: Using `head` on a potentially empty list will crash. Use pattern matching or `listToMaybe` to handle the empty case explicitly.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
getFirst :: [a] -> a
getFirst xs = head xs  -- crashes on []

getValue :: Map k v -> k -> v
getValue m k = fromJust (Map.lookup k m)  -- crashes on missing key

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
getFirst :: [a] -> Maybe a
getFirst [] = Nothing
getFirst (x:_) = Just x

getValue :: Map k v -> k -> Maybe v
getValue = Map.lookup

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Ban partial functions. Use total alternatives and let the type system enforce completeness.

items.text: Are deriving strategies (stock, newtype, anyclass, via) explicit?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The DerivingStrategies extension requires explicitly stating how each typeclass instance is derived, preventing ambiguity when multiple strategies could apply.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without explicit strategies, GHC picks one based on heuristics that can change between versions or when new instances are added, causing surprising behavior changes.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that DerivingStrategies is enabled in the project
- Look for deriving clauses without an explicit strategy keyword
- Verify that newtype deriving uses 'deriving newtype' rather than plain 'deriving'
baseContent.exampleComment: This `deriving (ToJSON)` is ambiguous — GHC could use DeriveAnyClass or GeneralizedNewtypeDeriving. Add an explicit strategy: `deriving anyclass (ToJSON)`.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
newtype UserId = UserId Int
  deriving (Show, ToJSON)

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
newtype UserId = UserId Int
  deriving stock (Show)
  deriving newtype (ToJSON)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use explicit deriving strategies to avoid ambiguity and surprises.

items.text: Do all top-level definitions have explicit type signatures?

baseContent.whatItMeans: While GHC can infer types, top-level definitions should have explicit type signatures that serve as documentation and catch unintended type changes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without type signatures, refactoring can silently change a function's type in ways that compile but are semantically wrong. Type signatures also serve as the primary documentation.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that all exported functions have type signatures
- Verify that -Wmissing-signatures is enabled in the build
- Look for top-level definitions without annotations
baseContent.exampleComment: This top-level function has no type signature. Add one for documentation and to catch unintended type changes during refactoring.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: processItems = map (\item -> item { status = Done })

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
processItems :: [Item] -> [Item]
processItems = map (\item -> item { status = Done })

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always write type signatures for top-level definitions. They are documentation that the compiler checks.

items.text: Are pattern matches exhaustive and do they avoid catch-all wildcards on sum types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Pattern matching on sum types should explicitly handle every constructor. Catch-all wildcards (_) hide newly added constructors from the compiler's exhaustiveness check.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When a new constructor is added to a sum type, catch-all wildcards silently handle it with the default case instead of generating a compile error. This defeats the purpose of sum types.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for wildcard patterns on sum types
- Check that -Wincomplete-patterns is enabled
- Verify that new constructors will cause compile errors in all relevant match sites
baseContent.exampleComment: This catch-all wildcard on PaymentStatus means adding a new status (e.g., Refunded) will silently fall through. Match each constructor explicitly.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
handleStatus :: PaymentStatus -> Text
handleStatus Paid = "Payment complete"
handleStatus _ = "Pending"  -- hides new constructors

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
handleStatus :: PaymentStatus -> Text
handleStatus Paid = "Payment complete"
handleStatus Pending = "Awaiting payment"
handleStatus Failed = "Payment failed"

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid catch-all wildcards on sum types. Explicit patterns make adding constructors a compile-time checked operation.

items.text: Is the monad transformer stack kept shallow and well-organized?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Monad transformer stacks (ReaderT config (ExceptT e (StateT s IO))) should be kept to 2-3 layers. Deep stacks require excessive lifting and become unreadable.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Deep transformer stacks cause O(n) performance overhead per bind, require manual lift calls, and make error messages incomprehensible.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Count the layers in the application monad stack
- Check for excessive lift or liftIO calls
- Verify that the stack is defined as a type alias for readability
baseContent.exampleComment: This 5-layer transformer stack is hard to work with. Consider using ReaderT + IO with an environment record, or an effect system like effectful.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
type App = ReaderT Config (ExceptT AppError (StateT Cache (WriterT [Log] IO)))

foo :: App ()
foo = do
  cfg <- ask
  lift $ lift $ modify (addEntry "key" "val")
  lift $ lift $ lift $ tell ["did thing"]

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer ReaderT + IO with an environment record over deep transformer stacks.

items.text: Is IO pushed to the edges, keeping core logic pure?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Business logic should be pure functions that take inputs and return outputs. IO operations (database, network, file system) should happen at the boundary and pass results to pure code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Pure functions are easier to test (no mocking), easier to reason about (no hidden effects), and easier to compose. IO at the edges makes the architecture cleaner.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if core business logic functions are in IO unnecessarily
- Look for functions that could take pure inputs instead of reading from the environment
- Verify that IO is limited to the top-level composition layer
baseContent.exampleComment: This business logic function is in IO but only uses IO to read a config value. Pass the config value as a parameter to make the function pure and testable.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
calculateDiscount :: OrderId -> IO Double
calculateDiscount oid = do
  order <- fetchOrder oid  -- IO
  rate <- readIORef discountRate  -- IO
  pure (orderTotal order * rate)  -- pure logic mixed with IO

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
calculateDiscount :: Double -> Order -> Double
calculateDiscount rate order = orderTotal order * rate  -- pure

-- IO at the boundary
main = do
  order <- fetchOrder oid
  rate <- readIORef discountRate
  let discount = calculateDiscount rate order

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Push IO to the edges. Pure core, impure shell.

items.text: Are typeclass constraints used instead of concrete monads for flexibility?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Functions should depend on typeclass constraints (MonadReader, MonadError) rather than concrete monad stacks, allowing different implementations for production and testing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Concrete monad types lock functions to a specific stack. Typeclass constraints allow swapping the implementation (e.g., using Identity for tests, IO for production).
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if functions use concrete App monad where typeclass constraints would suffice
- Look for functions that only need MonadReader but are typed as the full App stack
- Verify that test code can substitute lightweight monad implementations
baseContent.exampleComment: This function is typed as `App a` but only uses `ask`. Generalize to `MonadReader Config m => m a` so it can be tested without the full App stack.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
getDbUrl :: App String
getDbUrl = asks configDbUrl

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
getDbUrl :: MonadReader Config m => m String
getDbUrl = asks configDbUrl

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use typeclass constraints for flexibility. Test with simple monads, run with the real stack.

items.text: Are resources acquired and released using bracket or ResourceT?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Resources (file handles, database connections, sockets) must be acquired and released using bracket, finally, or ResourceT to ensure cleanup happens even when exceptions occur.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without bracket, exceptions between resource acquisition and release cause resource leaks. In long-running servers, this leads to file descriptor exhaustion or connection pool depletion.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for openFile, connect, acquire patterns without bracket
- Check that database connections use a pool with proper cleanup
- Verify that ResourceT or bracket is used for all resource acquisition
baseContent.exampleComment: This opens a file handle without bracket. If processFile throws, the handle leaks. Use `withFile` or `bracket` to ensure cleanup.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
readData :: FilePath -> IO Text
readData path = do
  h <- openFile path ReadMode
  content <- hGetContents h
  hClose h  -- not reached if hGetContents throws
  pure content

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
readData :: FilePath -> IO Text
readData path =
  withFile path ReadMode $ \h ->
    T.hGetContents h

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use bracket or withX functions for resource management. Manual acquire/release is a leak waiting to happen.

items.text: Are exceptions reserved for truly exceptional cases, with Either/ExceptT used for expected errors?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Expected errors (validation failures, not-found) should be modeled as Either/ExceptT in the return type. Runtime exceptions should be reserved for unexpected, unrecoverable failures.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Exceptions are invisible in the type signature, making it impossible to know what errors a function can produce without reading its implementation. Either makes errors explicit and composable.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for throwIO or throw for expected error conditions
- Check if validation errors are thrown as exceptions instead of returned as Either
- Verify that the error type hierarchy distinguishes recoverable from unrecoverable errors
baseContent.exampleComment: Throwing an exception for a missing user is not exceptional — it is an expected case. Return `Either UserNotFound User` instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
findUser :: UserId -> IO User
findUser uid = do
  mUser <- queryDb uid
  case mUser of
    Nothing -> throwIO (UserNotFound uid)  -- expected case!
    Just u -> pure u

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
findUser :: UserId -> IO (Either UserError User)
findUser uid = do
  mUser <- queryDb uid
  pure $ maybe (Left (UserNotFound uid)) Right mUser

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Either for expected errors and exceptions for unexpected ones. Make the error model explicit in the types.

items.text: Is unsafePerformIO avoided or thoroughly justified?

baseContent.whatItMeans: unsafePerformIO breaks the purity guarantee by performing IO inside a pure function. It should only be used in very specific, well-documented cases (e.g., global mutable references for caches).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Misuse of unsafePerformIO can cause data races, violate referential transparency, and produce bugs that are nearly impossible to reproduce or debug.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for unsafePerformIO, unsafeInterleaveIO, and unsafeDupablePerformIO
- Check that each usage has a comment explaining why it is safe
- Verify that the result is used as a top-level constant, not inside a function body
baseContent.exampleComment: This unsafePerformIO in a function body is dangerous — the compiler may inline or float it, causing the IO action to execute multiple times or not at all. Use IORef or MVar instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
getValue :: String -> String
getValue key = unsafePerformIO $ do
  readFile ("/config/" ++ key)  -- runs IO in "pure" code

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
getValue :: String -> IO String
getValue key = readFile ("/config/" ++ key)  -- honest about being IO

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid unsafePerformIO. If you think you need it, you probably need to restructure your code to be honest about effects.

items.text: Are accumulators and folds strict to prevent space leaks?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Lazy accumulators in folds build up large chains of unevaluated thunks that consume O(n) memory instead of O(1). Using foldl' (strict left fold) or bang patterns prevents this.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Space leaks are the most common Haskell performance issue. A lazy foldl on a million-element list can exhaust memory with thunks before producing any result.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for foldl (lazy) and check if foldl' (strict) should be used instead
- Look for accumulating parameters without bang patterns
- Check for Data.Map.Lazy vs Data.Map.Strict usage
baseContent.exampleComment: Using `foldl (+) 0` builds a chain of unevaluated additions. Use `foldl' (+) 0` from Data.List to force the accumulator at each step.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use strict folds (foldl') and bang patterns on accumulators. Lazy accumulation is almost never what you want.

items.text: Are data type fields strict where laziness is not needed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Record fields that will always be evaluated should use strict annotations (!) to prevent thunk accumulation. The StrictData extension makes all fields strict by default.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Lazy record fields accumulate thunks when the record is constructed but the field is not immediately needed. In long-lived data structures, this causes space leaks.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if StrictData is enabled for the project
- Look for data types with lazy fields that are always evaluated eventually
- Verify that intentionally lazy fields are marked with ~ (tilde) when StrictData is on
baseContent.exampleComment: These record fields are always evaluated when the record is used. Add bang patterns or enable StrictData to prevent thunk accumulation.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
data Stats = Stats
  { count :: Int     -- lazy: might be a thunk
  , total :: Double  -- lazy: might be a thunk
  , label :: Text    -- lazy: might be a thunk
  }

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
data Stats = Stats
  { count :: !Int     -- strict: always evaluated
  , total :: !Double  -- strict: always evaluated
  , label :: !Text    -- strict: always evaluated
  }

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to strict fields. Use laziness intentionally, not accidentally.

items.text: Are strict ByteString and Text used instead of lazy String?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Haskell's String type is a linked list of characters, which is extremely memory-inefficient. Data.Text and Data.ByteString provide packed, cache-friendly representations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: String uses ~40 bytes per character (due to list cons cells). Text uses ~2 bytes per character. For any real data processing, String is prohibitively slow and memory-hungry.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for uses of String in data types and function signatures
- Check that Data.Text is used for text processing and Data.ByteString for binary data
- Verify that -XOverloadedStrings is enabled to avoid manual pack/unpack calls
baseContent.exampleComment: This function uses String for file processing. Switch to Data.Text (or Data.ByteString for binary) for a 10-20x memory improvement.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
processFile :: FilePath -> IO [String]
processFile path = do
  content <- readFile path  -- lazy String IO
  pure (lines content)

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import qualified Data.Text as T
import qualified Data.Text.IO as TIO

processFile :: FilePath -> IO [T.Text]
processFile path = do
  content <- TIO.readFile path  -- strict Text IO
  pure (T.lines content)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use String for data processing. Text and ByteString are the correct default choices.

items.text: Is deepseq/NFData used to force full evaluation where needed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: seq only evaluates to weak head normal form (WHNF), which may leave nested thunks. deepseq evaluates the entire structure, which is necessary for timing, benchmarking, and avoiding deferred work.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using seq on a data structure only evaluates the outermost constructor. Nested fields can still contain thunks that shift work to unexpected places in the program.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that benchmarks use deepseq/force to fully evaluate results
- Look for cases where seq is used but nested thunks could remain
- Verify that NFData instances exist for types that need full evaluation
baseContent.exampleComment: This benchmark uses `seq` which only evaluates the outer constructor. Use `deepseq` or `force` to fully evaluate the result for accurate timing.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
benchmark :: [Int] -> IO ()
benchmark xs = do
  let result = expensiveComputation xs
  result `seq` pure ()  -- only evaluates outermost constructor

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import Control.DeepSeq (force)

benchmark :: [Int] -> IO ()
benchmark xs = do
  let result = force (expensiveComputation xs)  -- fully evaluated
  pure ()

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use deepseq when you need full evaluation. seq only scratches the surface.

items.text: Is streaming used for processing large data instead of loading it all into memory?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Processing large files or datasets should use streaming libraries (conduit, streaming, pipes) that process data in constant memory rather than loading everything into a list.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reading a large file into a list consumes memory proportional to the file size. Streaming processes data in constant memory, enabling processing of files larger than RAM.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for readFile calls on potentially large files
- Check if lists are used as intermediate data structures for large datasets
- Verify that streaming or chunked processing is used for IO-heavy operations
baseContent.exampleComment: This reads the entire CSV into memory as a list. Use conduit or streaming to process rows in constant memory.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
processCSV :: FilePath -> IO [Result]
processCSV path = do
  rows <- readAllRows path  -- entire file in memory
  pure (map transform rows)

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import Conduit

processCSV :: FilePath -> IO [Result]
processCSV path =
  runConduitRes
    $ sourceFile path
    .| decodeCSV
    .| mapC transform
    .| sinkList

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use streaming for large data processing. Lists are not a scalable data pipeline.

items.text: Are error types defined as custom sum types rather than raw strings?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Error conditions should be modeled as sum types with constructors for each case, not as String or Text messages. This enables exhaustive pattern matching on error cases.
baseContent.whyItMatters: String errors cannot be pattern-matched reliably and provide no type-level guidance about what can go wrong. Sum type errors make error handling exhaustive and compiler-checked.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for functions returning Either String or Either Text
- Check if error types are defined as sum types with descriptive constructors
- Verify that error types carry enough context for handling (not just a message)
baseContent.exampleComment: Using `Either String` makes it impossible to handle different error cases programmatically. Define a UserError sum type with constructors for each failure mode.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
createUser :: UserInput -> IO (Either String User)
createUser input
  | null (name input) = pure (Left "Name is required")
  | emailTaken = pure (Left "Email already exists")

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
data UserError
  = NameRequired
  | EmailAlreadyExists Email
  | InvalidEmail Text
  deriving (Show)

createUser :: UserInput -> IO (Either UserError User)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Model errors as sum types. Stringly-typed errors are not errors, they are surprises.

items.text: Is Either used instead of Maybe when the caller needs to know why something failed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Maybe signals presence or absence without explanation. Either provides a reason for failure. Functions should return Either when the caller needs to distinguish between failure modes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Returning Maybe when there are multiple failure modes forces callers to guess what went wrong. Either communicates the cause and enables informed error handling.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for functions returning Maybe that have multiple failure modes
- Check if callers of Maybe-returning functions need to know why they got Nothing
- Verify that Either is used when different error cases require different handling
baseContent.exampleComment: This returns Nothing for both invalid input and database errors. Use Either with a sum type to distinguish the failure modes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
findUser :: Text -> IO (Maybe User)
findUser email
  | not (isValidEmail email) = pure Nothing  -- why Nothing?
  | otherwise = queryDb email                 -- also returns Nothing

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
findUser :: Text -> IO (Either UserLookupError User)
findUser email
  | not (isValidEmail email) = pure (Left (InvalidEmail email))
  | otherwise = maybe (Left UserNotFound) Right <$> queryDb email

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Maybe for simple presence/absence. Use Either when the caller needs to know why it failed.

items.text: Do error values carry enough context for debugging?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Error constructors should include relevant data (the input that failed, the constraint that was violated) so that logs and error messages are actionable without additional investigation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An error like 'ValidationFailed' without context requires debugging to find which field failed and why. Including the field name and constraint in the error makes it immediately actionable.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that error constructors carry context (the failing value, field name, expected vs actual)
- Look for error types with no data fields (just bare constructors)
- Verify that Show instances for errors produce useful log messages
baseContent.exampleComment: The `InvalidAge` constructor carries no context. Include the actual value so the error message says 'InvalidAge: got -5, expected >= 0'.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: data ValidationError = InvalidAge | InvalidEmail | MissingField

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Include enough context in errors to debug without reproducing the issue.

items.text: Is async exception safety considered for critical sections?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Async exceptions (throwTo, timeout, cancel) can arrive between any two instructions. Critical sections that must complete atomically need protection with mask or uninterruptibleMask.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Async exceptions can interrupt resource cleanup, leave data in an inconsistent state, or cause deadlocks when they arrive inside lock acquisition sequences.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for multi-step resource operations that are not bracketed
- Check that bracket (which handles async exceptions) is used for resource management
- Verify that critical sections with locks use mask where needed
baseContent.exampleComment: This lock acquire/release sequence is not async-exception-safe. An async exception between acquire and release will leak the lock. Use bracket or mask.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
withLock :: MVar () -> IO a -> IO a
withLock lock action = do
  takeMVar lock       -- acquire
  result <- action    -- async exception here leaks the lock
  putMVar lock ()     -- release
  pure result

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
withLock :: MVar () -> IO a -> IO a
withLock lock = bracket_ (takeMVar lock) (putMVar lock ())

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use bracket for resource management. It handles async exceptions correctly by design.

items.text: Are errors logged with structured context before being re-thrown or returned?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When errors are caught, they should be logged with relevant context (request ID, input parameters, stack trace) before being re-thrown, wrapped, or returned to the caller.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Errors that propagate without logging are lost when caught at a higher level. Structured logging at the point of failure provides the most relevant context for debugging.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that catch/handle blocks log errors before re-throwing
- Verify that log messages include structured data (not just the error message)
- Look for silent error swallowing (catch without logging)
baseContent.exampleComment: This catch block re-throws without logging. Add structured logging with the request context before re-throwing so the error is traceable in production.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Log errors with context at the point of failure. Silent catch-and-rethrow loses valuable debugging information.

items.text: Do modules have explicit export lists?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Modules should explicitly list what they export rather than implicitly exporting everything. This controls the public API and hides implementation details.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without explicit exports, internal functions become part of the public API. Users depend on them, and refactoring internals becomes a breaking change.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that modules have explicit export lists: module Foo (Bar, baz) where
- Look for modules without export lists (which export everything)
- Verify that internal helper functions are not exported
baseContent.exampleComment: This module has no export list, exposing all internal helpers. Add an explicit export list to control the public API.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
module Database.User where  -- exports everything

internalHelper :: IO ()
findUser :: UserId -> IO (Maybe User)
createUser :: UserInput -> IO User

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
module Database.User
  ( findUser
  , createUser
  ) where

internalHelper :: IO ()  -- hidden
findUser :: UserId -> IO (Maybe User)
createUser :: UserInput -> IO User

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use explicit export lists. Control your public API surface.

items.text: Are imports qualified or explicit to avoid name clashes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Imports should be qualified (import qualified Data.Map as Map) or have explicit import lists (import Data.List (sort, nub)) to make it clear where each name comes from.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unqualified wildcard imports make it hard to tell where a function comes from, cause name clashes, and make code fragile when dependencies add new exports.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for unqualified imports without explicit import lists
- Check that commonly clashing modules (Data.Map, Data.Set, Data.Text) use qualified imports
- Verify that the import style is consistent across the project
baseContent.exampleComment: This unqualified import of Data.Map brings `map`, `filter`, and `lookup` into scope, clashing with Prelude. Use `import qualified Data.Map as Map`.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
import Data.Map
import Data.Text
import Data.ByteString

-- Which 'map' is this? Which 'length'?

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import qualified Data.Map.Strict as Map
import qualified Data.Text as T
import qualified Data.ByteString as BS

-- Map.lookup, T.length, BS.readFile — clear provenance

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use qualified imports for container and string modules. Make name provenance obvious.

items.text: Are implementation details placed in .Internal modules?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The Haskell convention is to place implementation details in Foo.Internal modules. These are accessible for testing and power users but signal that they are not part of the stable API.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Internal modules communicate API stability expectations. Users who depend on .Internal modules do so at their own risk, freeing library authors to refactor internals.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if implementation details are mixed into public-facing modules
- Look for opportunities to move helpers to .Internal modules
- Verify that .Internal modules are documented as unstable
baseContent.exampleComment: These internal parsing helpers are exported from the main module. Move them to Parser.Internal to signal that they are implementation details.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use .Internal modules for implementation details. They communicate stability guarantees.

items.text: Are orphan typeclass instances avoided?

baseContent.whatItMeans: An orphan instance is a typeclass instance defined in a module that owns neither the typeclass nor the type. Orphan instances cause coherence problems and unexpected behavior.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Orphan instances can silently conflict with instances defined elsewhere, causing unpredictable behavior depending on import order. They also break GHC's instance resolution assumptions.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for -Worphans warnings in the build output
- Look for instances defined in modules that do not own either the class or the type
- Verify that newtypes are used instead of orphan instances where appropriate
baseContent.exampleComment: This ToJSON instance for Data.Time.UTCTime is an orphan. Either use a newtype wrapper or move the instance to the module that defines the type.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
-- in MyApp.Instances (orphan!)
instance ToJSON UTCTime where
  toJSON = String . T.pack . show

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
-- Use a newtype to own the instance
newtype ApiTime = ApiTime UTCTime

instance ToJSON ApiTime where
  toJSON (ApiTime t) = String (T.pack (formatTime defaultTimeLocale "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ" t))

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid orphan instances. Use newtype wrappers to own the instance if you cannot modify the class or type modules.

items.text: Are language extensions and dependencies specified per-component in the cabal/package file?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Language extensions and package dependencies should be listed in the cabal file per component (library, executable, test-suite) rather than enabled globally or via pragma in every file.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Per-file LANGUAGE pragmas scatter configuration across hundreds of files. Cabal-level configuration provides a single source of truth and ensures consistency.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that commonly used extensions are in default-extensions in the cabal file
- Look for the same LANGUAGE pragma repeated across many files
- Verify that test dependencies are not leaked into the library component
baseContent.exampleComment: OverloadedStrings appears as a LANGUAGE pragma in 80% of source files. Add it to default-extensions in the cabal file to avoid repetition.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
library
  default-extensions:
    OverloadedStrings
    DeriveGeneric
    DerivingStrategies

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Put common extensions in the cabal file. Reserve per-file pragmas for module-specific extensions.

items.text: Is the Prelude choice documented and consistent across the project?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Many Haskell projects use an alternative Prelude (relude, rio, protolude) that provides safer defaults. The choice should be consistent and documented.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mixing Prelude choices within a project causes confusion and import conflicts. A documented choice helps new contributors understand the project conventions.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if the project uses an alternative Prelude
- Verify that the choice is configured in the cabal file (default-extensions: NoImplicitPrelude)
- Look for inconsistent Prelude usage across modules
baseContent.exampleComment: The project uses relude in most modules but this file uses the default Prelude. Add the standard import for consistency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pick a Prelude and stick with it. Document the choice for new contributors.
